summary: In this keynote, Jeff Dean discusses the exponential growth of machine learning research and its real-world applications, from autonomous vehicles to healthcare. He highlights the power of self-supervised learning (like BERT) and AutoML for automating model design. Finally, he outlines a future vision for AI: moving away from single-task models toward massive, sparsely activated, multi-task models that can dynamically route new tasks through existing expert components.

principles.statement: We should build single, multi-task models rather than separate models for every problem.
principles.rationale: A single, sparsely activated model can build on the knowledge of millions of tasks, allowing it to learn new tasks with significantly less data and computational cost by leveraging its existing expertise.
principles.source_quote: we tend to Train separate models for each different problem we care about I think this is a bit misguided like really we want one model that does a lot of things so that it can build on the knowledge in how it does thousands or millions of different things

principles.statement: Machine learning can distribute the wisdom of experts globally.
principles.rationale: By training models on data labeled by top specialists, we can package gold-standard expertise into a model and deploy it to regions suffering from severe shortages of trained professionals.
principles.source_quote: we can use machine learning to bring the wisdom of experts through a machine learning model anywhere in the world and that's really a huge huge opportunity

principles.statement: The growth of ideas has replaced the growth of computational power.
principles.rationale: While Moore's Law has slowed down, the machine learning research community is generating new ideas and efficiencies at an exponential rate, driving continued rapid progress.
principles.source_quote: we've replaced computational growth with growth of ideas and we'll see those both together will be important

principles.statement: High-quality data is the fundamental raw ingredient for machine learning.
principles.rationale: Even when individual human experts disagree or are inconsistent, aggregating multiple expert labels creates a high-quality dataset that allows off-the-shelf models to perform at or above expert levels.
principles.source_quote: like many machine learning problems high quality data is the right raw ingredient

frameworks.name: Self-Supervised Pre-training and Fine-tuning
frameworks.when_to_apply: When you have massive amounts of unlabeled data (like text) but very little labeled data for the specific task you care about.
steps: Pre-train a model on a massive amount of unlabeled data using a self-supervised objective (e.g., dropping 15% of words and predicting the missing ones).
Take the pre-trained model and fine-tune it using the small amount of labeled examples for your specific target task.
frameworks.source_quote: step one you can pre train a model on lots and lots of text using this fill in the blanks self supervisor objective function and then step two you can then take a language task you really care about... and now use your paltry 10,000 examples for the text task you really care about and that works extremely well

frameworks.name: Neural Architecture Search (AutoML)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When designing a neural network architecture for a specific task and wanting to automate the experimental process rather than relying on human intuition.
steps: Use a model-generating model to generate descriptions of different neural network architectures.
Train each generated model for a few hours.
Evaluate how well the models work on the target problem.
Use the accuracy of those models as a reinforcement learning signal to steer the model-generating model away from poor architectures and toward better ones.
Repeat the process many times to find highly effective, optimized architectures.
frameworks.source_quote: we're gonna train each of those for a few hours and then we're going to see how well they work and then use the accuracy of those models as a reinforcement learning signal for the model generating model to steer it away from models that didn't work very well

mental_models.name: Sparsely Activated Multi-Task Model (Neural Pathway Search)
mental_models.description: Viewing a machine learning model not as a monolithic block for one task, but as a vast network of specialized components. When a new task arrives, the system searches for a 'pathway' through existing components that already have relevant expertise, rather than activating the entire network or training from scratch.
mental_models.example: When a new task is introduced, the model routes the data through specific sub-components trained on related tasks, getting into a good state quickly. If needed, a new component is added to increase capacity for that specific task, which can then be reused later.

mental_models.name: AI as an Expert Multiplier
mental_models.description: Viewing AI not as a replacement for human workers, but as a distribution mechanism for highly specialized expertise to regions with severe shortages.
mental_models.example: Using a computer vision model trained by a consensus of retinal specialists to screen for diabetic retinopathy in India, where there is a shortage of 127,000 eye doctors.

heuristics: To resolve human labeler disagreement, get each example labeled by many experts and take the consensus to create high-quality training data.
Use self-supervised learning on abundant unlabeled data to make up for a lack of labeled data on specific tasks.

signature_quotes.text: we've replaced computational growth with growth of ideas
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how the exponential growth of ML research papers is making up for the slowing down of Moore's Law.

signature_quotes.text: we can use machine learning to bring the wisdom of experts through a machine learning model anywhere in the world and that's really a huge huge opportunity
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the potential of AI in advanced health informatics, specifically diabetic retinopathy screening.

signature_quotes.text: we tend to Train separate models for each different problem we care about I think this is a bit misguided like really we want one model that does a lot of things
signature_quotes.context: Explaining his vision for the future of machine learning architectures.

signature_quotes.text: the notion that deep neural networks are sort of complete black boxes it's a bit overdone
signature_quotes.context: Discussing advances in model explainability, particularly in healthcare where clinicians need to understand predictions.

anti_patterns.description: Training separate, isolated models for every single problem we care about.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents models from building on the knowledge of how to do thousands of other things, requiring more data and compute for every new task instead of leveraging existing expertise.

anti_patterns.description: Treating deep neural networks as complete black boxes.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the development of explainability techniques (like highlighting relevant parts of input data) that are crucial for clinical and real-world adoption.
